examples.text: بِنَفْسِي الْخَيَالُ الزَّائِرِي بَعْدَ هَجْعَةٍ / وَقَوْلَتُهُ لِي: بَعْدَنَا الْغُمْضَ تَطْعَمُ؟

body.text: image; such as that which is imaged in sleep,
any subtile thing of a similar kind:
anything that one sees like a shadow:
a thing that sometimes passes by one, resembling a shadow:
both signify the same; (JK, S, K;) i. e.
an apparition; a phantom; a spectre; a fancied image; an imaginary form;
a form that is seen in sleep
anything that one sees like a shadow
[as the former word is explained above]; and
a thing that is seen in sleep;
a form that is imaged to one in the mind when awake,
(K:) the former word is both masc. and fem.: (Ham p. 316:) pl.
also, as a pl. of mult., mentioned as one of the pls. of
in another sense, below]. You say,
became imaged to me in my mind
]. (Msb, TA.) And a poet says, (S,) namely, El-Bohturee, (TA,)

falasotu binaAzilK A_il~aA A^alam~ato
                      
                     
                        Alka*uwbu ↓ 
      biraHoliY A^awo xayaAlatuhaA
And I do not alight but she visits my abode, or her false apparition

A faculty that retains what the fancy perceives of the forms of objects of sense after the substance has become absent, so that the fancy beholds them whenever it turns towards them: thus it is the store-house of the fancy: its place is the hinder part of the first venter of
the three which are comprised by
(KT. [In this sense, it is incorrectly written in Freytag's Lex. (in which only the Arabic words of the explanation are given, preceded by the rendering “ phantasia, ”)
The shadow of himself which a bird sees when rising into the sky;
whereupon he pounces down upon it, thinking it to be a prey, and finds it to be nothing: he is [the bird] called
figure which one sees from a distance,
, (S, K,) of a man; and his
A piece of wood with black garments upon it,
which is set up to make the beasts and birds fancy it to be a man:
a piece of wood with a garment thrown upon it, which is set up for the sake of the sheep or goats, in order that the wolf, seeing it, may think it to be a man:

A^axiY laA A^axaA liY gayoruhu gayora A^an~aniY
                      
                     
                        karaAEiY xayaAlK yasotaTiyfu bilaAfikori
(S, TA) [cited by J as an ex. of
in the former of the senses explained in the sentence immediately preceding: but the meaning seems to be,
My brother: I have no brother but he: but I am like one watching an image dressed up to decoy; going round about without reflection:
the young ostrich for which the sportsman sets up a
an image dressed up to decoy
in order that it may become familiar therewith, and the sportsman may then take it, and the young ostrich may follow him.
A thing that is set up in land in order that it may be known to be prohibited to the public, and may not be approached.
